Empires & Puzzles Xiaoqing Review: The Lunar Snake Healer

Is this snake playing a drum or just beating the odds? We review Xiaoqing, the Lunar New Year healer with a penchant for distributing ailments and reflecting bad vibes. Find out if he's a slithering success or a percussion percussion failure in today's meta.
Xiaoqing, The Lunar Snake: Healer, Reflector, and Minion Silencer

Xiaoqing is a 5-star Legendary Nature (Green) hero from the Lunar New Year event, released in January 2025 to celebrate the Year of the Snake. As a Cleric, he fits naturally into a support role, offering healing, cleansing utility, and a unique ailment reflection mechanic. While his arrival brought festive cheer, players quickly noticed his kit feels like a mix of powerful utility and cautious design—a hero who wants to do it all but doesn't quite commit to being a master of one trade.

The artwork for Xiaoqing is certainly... distinct. We have a green snake, adorned in festive attire, seemingly playing a drum with the tip of its tail. It’s a whimsical design that leans heavily into the "Lunar New Year" aesthetic, though some might find the logistics of a snake playing percussion a bit puzzling. Compared to the fierce warriors or terrifying monsters usually gracing our screens, Xiaoqing looks like he's ready to lead a parade rather than a warband. It's a refreshing change of pace, even if it does look a bit like he's auditioning for a very specific niche in a marching band.

, he sits significantly behind the current wave of Construct, Astral, and Super Elemental heroes who are pushing 1260-1280 base power.

In summary, his stats are "good enough" to be usable, but he won't be winning any awards for raw bulk or power. He relies heavily on his skills to make an impact.

This is the bread and butter. 40% is a solid chunk of health, standard for average speed healers. While it doesn't offer the overheal (boosted health) that is prevalent in the current meta, it's reliable sustain.

cleansable ailment from allies and dumps it onto enemies. This is a fascinating mechanic. It's not a full cleanse, which can be a liability against ailment-heavy teams (like those stacking burns, poisons, and defense down). However, turning a dangerous defense down or mindless attack back onto the enemy can be a game-changer if timed perfectly.

This is the most controversial part of his kit. All allies get a 60% chance to reflect status ailments for 3 turns. When it works, it's hilarious and effective. When it fails (which is 40% of the time), your team takes the full brunt of the attack. In a game where consistency is king, a coin-flip mechanic on a 5-star hero's main defensive buff feels underwhelming.

60% is statistically better than a coin flip, but compared to Toon passives (75% resist/reflect) or Elemental Reflects (100%), it feels risky. Over 10 incoming ailments, you statistically reflect 6.
Family Bonus, Passives, Aether Power & Unique Abilities

This passive was once a godsend against the likes of Waterpipe or Miriam & Midnight. It stops standard minions from using their special effects (like mana cuts or hits).
Crucially, this does NOT affect Mega Minions.
With the rise of Mega Minions in late 2025, this passive has lost significant value, though it still hard-counters classic minion summoners.
A solid defensive choice. Reducing all incoming damage by a flat percentage helps mitigate his aging defense stat and ensures he lives long enough to fire his heal.
Released during the 2025 Lunar New Year, Xiaoqing was seen as a "balanced" hero—never broken, but useful for rosters lacking premium Nature healers.
One year later, he has struggled to keep up. The introduction of heroes with 1300+ stats, Overheal, and 100% reflect mechanics has pushed him to the fringes.

hero. He is still excellent for wars (Cleanse Aid or Rush) and for newer players who need a 5-star healer. However, in top 100 play, the reliance on a 60% RNG reflect and the inability to counter Mega Minions makes him a risky pick. He is a "Use if you have him, don't chase him" hero.
As a healer, Xiaoqing's primary job is to survive and keep the team alive.

If Xiaoqing is your first 5-star healer, level him immediately. He will carry you through map stages and early raids. The 40% heal is massive in the early game, and the reflect can cheese difficult bosses.

Xiaoqing is likely a "depth" hero for your roster. He's useful for War Cleanups or specifically countering teams with standard minions (Waterpipe/M&M). Do not prioritize Limit Breaking him over top-tier meta healers like Hathor or newer Construct healers. He does not warrant Alpha Aethers unless you really love the art.
The community reaction to Xiaoqing was... whelmed.
One user on the forum lamented,
"I don’t know why he only got a 60% chance to give back ailments... That’s a nonsense."
This sentiment was echoed by many who felt a modern 5-star should have consistency.
"He is the poor snake’s version of Revna,"
highlighting that other heroes do the "ailment return" mechanic better or more aggressively.
On the flip side, some players defended him:
"Stats are solid, healing is ok... and the minion deactivation passive is a welcome addition."
The consensus settled on him being a "B-grade hero that could have easily been a solid A," held back by the RNG of his reflect and the "latest ailment only" restriction.

Xiaoqing is a hero who tries to be a jack-of-all-trades but ends up being a master of "maybe." Maybe he reflects that defense down, maybe he doesn't. Maybe he lifts the worst ailment, maybe he just lifts a minor one. He brings a festive drum to a battlefield dominated by laser cannons. While he is a capable healer and offers decent utility against specific enemies, he lacks the consistency and raw power to be a staple in the modern era.
If you have him, use him for the heal and enjoy the occasional hilarious moment where he reflects a game-ending ailment. Just don't bet your war score on that 60% chance.
A solid B-Tier hero. Reliable healer, unreliable reflector.
